policy_statement: For waste, Hiroshima City sets KPIs including reducing waste per person per day to 826 g/person/day by FY2020 and reducing incineration amount to 295,000 ton/year by FY2020.
mechanism_description: It sets KPIs to reduce per-person waste generation and the amount of waste incinerated, lowering emissions associated with waste treatment.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste generation/treatment activity → ↓ incineration/processing emissions (and avoided upstream waste-related emissions) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: A city sets quantitative KPIs/targets to reduce per-capita waste generation and the quantity of waste incinerated (often implying increased prevention, reuse, recycling, and alternative treatment).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Less waste is collected/processed and less is combusted; this reduces direct CO₂ (and other climate-relevant gases) from combustion and lowers energy/fuel use in waste handling and treatment operations.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions attributable to municipal solid waste management.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from waste treatment (especially avoided incineration and upstream avoided waste handling).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central causal chain is “set waste reduction/incineration reduction targets → reduce waste treated/combusted → reduce waste-sector GHG emissions,” which is a direct emissions mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although waste reduction can also improve resource efficiency (using fewer materials) and reduce local pollutants, the stated mechanism and climate-relevant endpoint here is explicitly lowering emissions from waste treatment; thus Mitigation dominates.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a target is framed purely around material circularity without a clear emissions endpoint, it could be argued as Resource Efficiency; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly links targets to waste treatment emissions reduction, keeping Mitigation primary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy sharing this mechanism (targets to reduce waste generation and/or incineration to cut treatment emissions) has its largest climate impact through reduced GHG emissions from waste operations, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Any secondary classification (e.g., whether benefits are mainly from avoided incineration CO₂ vs avoided methane via diversion, or from upstream material production savings) requires policy-level and system-boundary evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If reduced incineration is replaced by increased landfilling of organics without methane capture, net GHG benefits may be smaller or ambiguous; classification remains Mitigation because the intended dominant pathway is emissions reduction from waste treatment, but outcome depends on the disposal shift and accounting boundary.
key_indicators: "Key Performance Indicator (KPI)"; "Waste amount per day per person"; "Incineration amount of waste"; "826 g/person/day by FY2020"; "295,000 ton/year by FY2020"; baseline figures "(FY2013)"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants from incineration; lower waste management costs; improved resource efficiency/recycling outcomes.
instance_edge_case_notes: Targets/KPIs are stated without the accompanying implementation measures (e.g., pricing, mandates, infrastructure), so classification is limited to target-setting rather than a direct regulatory or investment instrument.
